plan.contextChunk:
**Answer**: Yes! One example is the Gekko's eye. Their pupil has a four-diamond-shaped pinhole aperture that could allow them to encode distance to a target in the retinal image @Murphy1986. Photo credit: @Randall.

![](figures/simplesystem/Tokay-Gecko-Eye-1440x954.jpg){width="90%"}
:::

### A Catalog of Edges

Edges denote image regions where there are strong changes of the image
with respect to location. Those variations can be due to a multitude of
scene factors (e.g., occlusion boundaries, changes in surface
orientation, changes in surface albedo, shadows).

![Edges denote image regions where there are sharp changes of the image
intensities. Those variations can be due to a multitude of scene factors
(e.g., occlusion boundaries, changes in surface orientation, changes in
surface albedo, and shadows).](figures/simplesystem/edgeLabeling.png){#fig-edgeLabeling
width="90%"}

One of the tasks that we will solve first is to classify **image edges**
according to their most probable cause. We will use the following
classification of image boundaries (@fig-edgeLabeling):

-   **Object boundaries**: These indicate pixels that delineate the
    boundaries of any object. Boundaries between objects generally
    correspond to changes in surface color, texture, and orientation.

-   Changes in **surface orientation**: These indicate locations where
    there are strong image variations due to changes in the surface
    orientations. A change in surface orientation produces changes in
    the image intensity because intensity is a function of the angle
    between the surface and the incident light.

-   **Shadow edges**: This can be harder than it seems. In this simple
    world, shadows are soft, creating slow transitions between dark and
    light.


[...]

pseudoinverse to compute the solution: 

$$\begin{aligned}
\bf Y = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}$$ This problem can be solved efficiently as the matrix
$\mathbf{A}$ is very sparse (most of the elements are zero).

### Results

@fig-worldCoordinates shows the resulting world coordinates $X(x,y)$,
$Y(x,y)$, $Z(x,y)$ for each pixel. The world coordinates are shown here
as images with the gray level coding the value of each coordinate (black
corresponds to the value 0).

![The solution to our vision problem for the input image of
@fig-edgeLabeling. World coordinates $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ are shown as
images.](figures/simplesystem/worldCoordinates.png){#fig-worldCoordinates
width="100%"}

There are a few things to reflect on:

-   It works. At least it seems to work pretty well. Knowing how well it
    works will require having some way of evaluating performance. This
    will be important.

-   But it cannot possibly work all the time. We have made lots of
    assumptions that will work only in this simple world. The rest of
    the book will involve upgrading this approach to apply to more
    general input images.

Despite that this approach will not work on general images, many of the

elements: edges labeled by occlusion, change of surface orientation, contact edge, shadow boundary

interactions.teaches: By changing the viewing angle, you can see how different edges reveal themselves based on perspective and lighting.

interactions.label: Show shadows
interactions.teaches: Shows how shadows affect visible edges and emphasize object boundaries.

demo_steps.title: Exploring Surface Orientation
demo_steps.narration: As you orbit around the scene, pay attention to how the edges on the blue block indicate changes in surface orientation. The lighting highlights the shifts in the block's geometry.

demo_steps.title: Identify Occlusion Edges
demo_steps.narration: Notice the occlusion edges as you change the viewpoint, particularly where one block hides another. These edges mark where one object visually interrupts another.

demo_steps.title: Shadow Effects
demo_steps.narration: Toggle the shadows off and on to see how they define shadow boundaries and impact the perception of edges and depth.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Start with a slightly elevated viewpoint looking down at the blocks to showcase the shadow boundaries clearly.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact sliders/toggles, visible near an edge with no filled panel, and must not cover important geometry
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Use smooth shading for the blocks and soft shadows to visually differentiate the types of edges. Shadows should be dynamically calculated based on the light source position relative to the camera.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera angle differs, causing the figures to be viewed from a different perspective.
The blue block colors look darker than in the source image.
The edges in the generated screenshot are not as pronounced.
The labels are smaller and less visible compared to the source figure.
Aspect ratio seems stretched; objects are slightly elongated.
evaluation.notes: The 3D layout generally matches the source, but issues with camera perspective and label clarity hinder faithfulness.
action_items: Adjust the camera position to better match the source figure's perspective.
Ensure the color saturation and brightness more closely mimic the source image.
Improve the visibility and size of labels to enhance readability.

feedback.rationale: The concept is accurately captured, but execution-related issues like camera position, color, and label size need improvements, which require refinement in generation.

Ensure the color saturation and brightness more closely mimic the source image.
